30: ## 0. What just shipped (live, verified today)
31: 
32: Khala is live as an **OpenAI-compatible inference API**:
33: 
34: - **Endpoint:** `POST https://openagents.com/api/v1/chat/completions`, single public
35:   model id **`openagents/khala`** (the `khala-mini` / `khala-code` ids are not
36:   public products; see `../khala/README.md` and the §6228 free-tier module
37:   header in `apps/openagents.com/workers/api/src/inference/inference-free-tier-key.ts`).
38: - **Free tier, self-serve:** `POST /api/keys/free` mints a normal `oa_agent_`
39:   bearer key; free inference on `openagents/khala` is metered against a per-key
40:   per-UTC-day quota of **200 requests / 200,000 tokens** (`FREE_TIER_MAX_REQUESTS_PER_DAY`,
41:   `FREE_TIER_MAX_TOKENS_PER_DAY` in `inference-free-tier-key.ts`). Over-quota or
42:   premium lanes fall through to the normal balance + `402` gate — free within the
43:   quota, paid beyond it.
44: - **A live public "Khala Tokens Served" counter + per-day history:**
45:   `GET /api/public/khala-tokens-served` and
46:   `GET /api/public/khala-tokens-served/history` (registered in `index.ts`, backed
47:   by the token-usage ledger). The recorder increments on every completion;
48:   verified to the token under 24-wide concurrent stress. Shown on `/stats` and
49:   `/khala`.
50: 
51: This is the buy-side OpenAgents has historically never closed (the Ep 138 "lots
52: of sell-side, no paying demand" lesson, restated in
53: `2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md`). The free OpenAI-compatible API plus a
54: public counter is the first time we have a real, measurable demand surface that an
55: outsider can point an existing tool at in one config line.
56: 
57: ## 1. The thesis
58: 
59: **We are now in the inference business. The one metric that matters is tokens
60: served per day, and we want it exponential.**
61: 
62: Episode 242 (`../transcripts/242.md`) named the shape: an OpenAI-compatible API
63: people already know how to consume, one model `openagents/khala`, free research
64: preview live today, an open marketplace underneath, and any paid value fanned to
65: contributors. The collective-intelligence essay
66: (`../collective-intelligence/2026-06-24-collective-intelligence-as-an-economy.md`)
67: named the mechanism: an **economy** selected by verifiable value, not a lab
68: artifact graded on its own benchmark. The inference business docs
69: (`2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md`,
70: `2026-06-19-pricing-model.md`) named the money: credits, multipliers, BTC
71: discount, three-way split (margin / serving node / referrer).
72: 
73: Tokens served per day is the metric that subsumes all of them:
74: 
75: - It is the **demand** proxy (the buy-side closing).
76: - It is the **dogfood** proxy (every internal system we route through Khala adds
77:   tokens).
78: - It is the **distribution** proxy (every ecosystem tool we land adds tokens).
79: - It is the **economy** proxy — once the paid loop is collectable, tokens served
80:   is what the three-way split is computed over.
81: 
82: Everything below is in service of making that one number go up, fast, **honestly**
83: (real served tokens, not vanity inflation), and **well** (good enough that people
84: keep their tools pointed at us).
85: 
86: We do not need to invent demand for inference — it is already paid for elsewhere
87: (OpenRouter, Fireworks, Together, Factory). We need to (a) be trivially adoptable,
88: (b) be good, and (c) generate our own demand from everything we already run.
89: 
90: ## 2. Pillar 1 — The flywheel: plug Khala into EVERYTHING we build/run
91: 
92: Eat our own dog food. Each internal use is **both** dogfood (it hardens Khala and
93: surfaces real traffic shapes for benchmarking) **and** demand (it adds tokens to
94: the counter that we control and can grow on day one, before a single external
95: user arrives). This is the fastest, most honest lever: it is real served tokens
96: from real work.
97: 
98: Targets, in rough order of how much traffic they can move:
99: 
100: 1. **Autonomous QA (`qa-runner` → Khala).** The autonomous QA process (the
101:    out-ship-Factory QA epic; the 242 draft calls QA "the first real use case")
102:    does real browser work and leaves green VERIFIED traces. Route its agent
103:    inference through `openagents/khala`. QA runs continuously, so it is a steady
104:    token floor and a continuous correctness signal on Khala itself. **Highest-value
105:    first move:** it is already running, it is internal, and it stress-tests the
106:    exact code/verification workload Khala is meant to be good at.
107: 2. **OpenAgents agents (Autopilot / Raynor) use Khala.** Autopilot is the gateway's
108:    designated anchor buyer (`2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md` §2): every
109:    coding session is captive first-party demand. Raynor's forum/progress posting and
110:    any in-product agent reasoning should default to `openagents/khala` where the
111:    model fit allows. Coding is the wedge.
112: 3. **All our products route inference through Khala.** Sites generation, forum
113:    agent flows, onboarding programs, the Artanis loop, Concierge — anywhere a
114:    product currently calls a model directly, make Khala the default lane (with the
115:    honest caveat that `claude`/premium lanes still route to the balance+premium
116:    gate, never the free lane — see the free-tier module's lane policy). One
117:    internal seam, many internal callers, all counted.
118: 4. **The gym trains Khala AND uses Khala.** The training/gym loop is dual-purpose:
119:    it improves the model that backs `openagents/khala`, and its own agent/eval
120:    inference can run *through* Khala. Training that consumes the product it
121:    improves is the tightest possible flywheel — and it ties directly to the
122:    "improves, does not depreciate" claim from Ep 242. (Direction: the gym↔Khala
123:    wiring is not a single shipped seam today; treat as the next dogfood lane to
124:    build, not a live claim.)
125: 5. **The 3D Verse visualization uses Khala.** `../khala/khala-in-the-world.md`
126:    renders every request to the endpoint as crackling energy fanned to assigned
127:    Pylons. Driving Verse NPC / scene / narration inference through Khala makes the
128:    visualization literally show its own traffic, and adds tokens. (Direction.)
129: 
130: **Why this is the right first pillar:** it is the only lever we fully control. We
131: can move the counter meaningfully *before* a single external developer adopts us,
132: and every internal token is a real test that makes the product better for the
133: external developers we are about to court in Pillar 2. Honesty note: internal
134: dogfood tokens are real served tokens and may be reported as such, but we should
135: be able to **distinguish** internal vs external demand in our own analytics so we
136: never imply external traction we do not have.
137: 
138: ## 3. Pillar 2 — The ecosystem-tools playbook
139: 
140: This is exactly how every inference business grew. OpenRouter, Fireworks, and
141: Together did not win by being a website — they won by being a **drop-in base URL +
142: key + model id** in the tools developers already use, so adoption costs one config
143: line and zero rewrites. Our gateway is OpenAI-compatible *specifically* so this
144: works (`2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md` §4: "work by changing only the
145: base URL + key — zero-rewrite adoption removes friction").
146: 
147: The play: get `openagents/khala` listed/documented as a provider or preset in the
148: popular coding + agent tools, so anyone can point an existing tool at
149: `https://openagents.com/api/v1` and run.
150: 
151: ### First target: OpenCode (`projects/repos/opencode`)
152: 
153: OpenCode is a provider-agnostic coding agent that reads a JSON config and supports
154: **custom OpenAI-compatible providers** via the AI SDK. Making it trivial to run
155: coding agents through OpenCode against our endpoint is the cleanest first landing:
156: it is a coding tool (our wedge), it is config-driven (no upstream PR needed to
157: start), and it exercises the same code/tool-calling workload Khala must be good at.
158: 
159: **Exact config** (verified against the OpenCode repo docs/schema —
160: `packages/web/src/content/docs/providers.mdx`, `config.mdx`,
161: `packages/core/src/config/provider.ts`). Put in `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json`
162: (global) or `opencode.json` (project root):
163: 
164: ```json
165: {
166:   "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
167:   "provider": {
168:     "openagents": {
169:       "npm": "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
170:       "name": "OpenAgents",
171:       "options": {
172:         "baseURL": "https://openagents.com/api/v1",
173:         "apiKey": "{env:OPENAGENTS_API_KEY}"
174:       },
175:       "models": {
176:         "openagents/khala": {
177:           "name": "Khala",
178:           "limit": { "context": 128000, "output": 65536 }
179:         }
180:       }
181:     }
182:   }
183: }
184: ```
185: 
186: - **Base URL:** `https://openagents.com/api/v1`. `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` POSTs
187:   to `<baseURL>/chat/completions`, i.e. our live `POST /api/v1/chat/completions`.
188: - **Key:** mint one free with `POST /api/keys/free`, export it as
189:   `OPENAGENTS_API_KEY`, or use OpenCode's `/connect → Other → id "openagents"` to
190:   store it in `~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json`. The provider id typed in
191:   `/connect` must equal the `provider` key in the config.
192: - **Model selection:** OpenCode's model reference is `providerId/modelKey`. With
193:   the model **key** `openagents/khala` (which is what gets sent upstream as the
194:   model id — correct for our API), the in-TUI selector path reads
195:   `openagents/openagents/khala`. **TBD to confirm in testing:** whether to keep the
196:   model key literally `openagents/khala` (sends the right model id; selector has a
197:   doubled segment) or to add server-side acceptance of a shorter key so the
198:   selector reads cleanly. Document whichever we choose; do not ship ambiguous
199:   instructions.
200: 
201: **What to test before we publish the OpenCode recipe:**
202: - The endpoint serves chat-completions at `/api/v1/chat/completions` (it does) and
203:   OpenCode's request reaches it with `model: "openagents/khala"`.
204: - **Tool/function calling** works end to end — OpenCode's edit/run loop depends on
205:   it. This is the single biggest risk: a model that cannot reliably tool-call is
206:   unusable as a coding agent regardless of token quality.
207: - **Streaming (SSE)** works for a good interactive experience.
208: - The free-tier 200 req / 200k tok daily quota is enough for a real "try it"
209:   session, and the `402`/quota-exceeded fall-through is a clean, legible error in
210:   OpenCode (not an opaque crash).
211: - Token accounting: an OpenCode session's tokens show up on the public
212:   `khala-tokens-served` counter (proving external traffic is counted).
213: 
214: ### Next tools after OpenCode
215: 
216: Prioritized by how directly each is a one-config-line OpenAI-compatible drop-in
217: and how much coding/agent traffic it represents (reference repos under
218: `projects/repos/`):
219: 
220: 1. **Aider** (`projects/repos/aider`) — CLI coding agent; OpenAI-compatible base
221:    URL + model env config. Big, well-known coding audience.
222: 2. **Cline / Continue** — VS Code agent extensions with custom OpenAI-compatible
223:    provider config. Large IDE-native audiences.
224: 3. **Vercel AI SDK** (`projects/repos/ai`) — `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` provider;
225:    a documented snippet lets every AI-SDK app point at us. This is the substrate
226:    under OpenCode and many others, so a clean AI-SDK recipe is high-leverage.
227: 4. **LiteLLM / LangChain provider lists** — getting `openagents/khala` into the
228:    community provider/config lists (LiteLLM `model_list`, LangChain
229:    `ChatOpenAI(base_url=...)`) reaches the long tail of agent frameworks.
230: 5. **Codex / Claude Code-compatible clients** — any client that already speaks the
231:    OpenAI Chat Completions shape and accepts a base URL override (per the gateway
232:    business doc's named clients).
233: 6. **OpenRouter-style aggregators** — listing Khala as an upstream model in
234:    aggregators that fan out to many providers (later-stage; needs reliability +
235:    the paid loop).
236: 
237: For each tool the deliverable is the same: a short, exact, copy-pasteable recipe
238: (base URL + free key + model id) plus a "what to test" checklist, published where
239: the tool's users look. Keep the recipes in this repo's docs and on `/khala` once
240: the copy gate clears.
241: 
242: ## 4. Pillar 3 — Benchmarking ("make it good," not just "get there")
243: 
244: Adoption dies if the model is not good. We need a repeatable benchmarking process
245: that measures Khala's **quality + latency + cost** against other tools/models, so
246: we can improve it deliberately and so our public comparisons are receipts, not
247: vibes.
248: 
249: We already have the foundation. The typed, fixture-driven, no-spend benchmark
250: harness shipped under
251: `apps/openagents.com/workers/api/src/inference/benchmark/`
252: (`../khala/2026-06-23-khala-benchmark-harness-book-p1-5.md`): a declarative
253: matrix (lane × engine × workload × sequence-shape × transport × verification
254: outcome), a runner with a fixture lane (deterministic, spend-free) and an
255: **owner-gated** real lane (`makeRealLaneSeam`, default OFF, refuses to spend
256: unarmed), and a public-safe dereferenceable report (latency percentiles,
257: **cost-per-accepted-outcome**, verification rate, cache hit rate). The book's
258: lesson is baked in: "faster" is meaningless until you say faster at *what*, on
259: *which lane*, under *which traffic*, judged on *which outcome*; read latency in
260: P50/P90/P99, not the mean; and a benchmark is only decision-grade when an
261: owner-armed real seam runs over **realistic** traffic.
262: 
263: What the GTM push needs on top of that:
264: 
265: - **An owner-armed real sweep.** Replace synthetic shapes with shapes sourced from
266:   observed Khala traffic (the dogfood from Pillar 1 *is* that traffic), arm
267:   `makeRealLaneSeam` with a live executor under a budget cap, and produce the first
268:   `decisionGrade: true` report comparing Khala vs Fireworks/Vertex on chat /
269:   khala-code / verifier / long-context. This is the existing Open Question #5
270:   minimum decision suite, run for real. (Owner-gated; not done in this doc.)
271: - **External head-to-head comparisons.** Extend beyond our own lanes to compare
272:   `openagents/khala` against the tools/models developers would otherwise use, on
273:   the same prompts, reporting tokens, $, wall-clock, **and** our unique axes
274:   (cost-per-accepted-outcome, verified-rate). This is the buildout roadmap's
275:   north-star "Fugu-vs-frontier"-style head-to-head
276:   (`../khala/khala-buildout-roadmap.md`), but generalized into a recurring quality
277:   bar we can publish.
278: 
279: ### Run it through the gym — the benchmark ladder
280: 
281: The **gym** is where this runs: the same gym that trains and uses Khala (Pillar 1)
282: also **benchmarks** it on a recurring basis, so every training or serving change is
283: automatically re-scored. The gym drives the harness above over a *ladder of
284: opponents*, all on identical prompts and our axes (tokens, $, wall-clock,
285: cost-per-accepted-outcome, verified-rate):
286: 
287: 1. **BigPickle vs Khala** — the first named baseline target. *(Confirm exactly
288:    which model/benchmark "BigPickle" refers to before wiring this rung; the rest
289:    of the ladder doesn't depend on it.)*
290: 2. **Other (open / free) models vs Khala** — the open models a developer would
291:    otherwise reach for (the catalog/ecosystem set), so we know where Khala stands
292:    among the free options people compare us to.
293: 3. **Paid (frontier) models vs Khala** — the paid Claude/GPT/Gemini-class models as
294:    the upper bar, so we can see the gap to frontier and track it closing over time.
295: 
296: The progression is deliberate: prove Khala against the baseline and the **free**
297: field first (where we must win on cost-per-accepted-outcome and verified-rate),
298: then measure the climb toward **paid** frontier. The gym publishes the ladder as a
299: recurring, dereferenceable leaderboard — and per the honesty bar below, numbers are
300: only published from the owner-armed real seam over realistic traffic
301: (`decisionGrade: true`).
302: 
303: Honesty bar for any published benchmark number: it must come from the owner-armed
304: real seam over realistic traffic (`decisionGrade: true`); fixture/synthetic runs
305: are explicitly labeled illustrative and never published as measurements.
306: 
307: ## 5. Sequence
308: 
309: 1. **Internal dogfood demand (Pillar 1), starting now.** Route qa-runner →
310:    Khala, then Autopilot/Raynor, then the rest of our products. This moves the
311:    counter immediately and generates the realistic traffic the benchmark needs.
312:    It is the only step that depends on nobody but us.
313: 2. **OpenCode integration (Pillar 2, first target).** Verify and publish the exact
314:    one-config recipe + test checklist. First external "point your tool at us" win.
315: 3. **Benchmark harness / gem (Pillar 3).** Confirm the owner's benchmark+competitor
316:    names; arm a real sweep over the now-real dogfood traffic; produce the first
317:    decision-grade report and a publishable head-to-head.
318: 4. **Broaden to more tools (Pillar 2 continued).** Aider → Cline/Continue → Vercel
319:    AI SDK → LiteLLM/LangChain lists → aggregators. One recipe at a time, each with
320:    a test checklist.
321: 5. **Keep driving internal dogfood demand throughout.** Pillar 1 never stops; new
322:    internal systems default to Khala as they ship. Constant motion.
323: 
324: These overlap (1 and 2 can run in parallel; 1 feeds 3), but the *gating* order is:
325: internal demand and OpenCode do not block on the paid loop or the registry, so
326: they ship first; the paid three-way-split economics are owner-gated and sequence
327: behind proof.
328: 
329: ## 6. Metrics + honesty
330: 
331: **The north-star metric is tokens served per day** — read off the live
332: `/api/public/khala-tokens-served` counter and its `/history`, the same numbers
333: shown on `/stats` and `/khala`. We want the per-day history curve to bend upward
334: and stay up. Supporting metrics:
335: 
336: - **Per-tool adoption** — tokens attributable to OpenCode, Aider, etc. (so we know
337:   which ecosystem landings actually move traffic).
338: - **Internal vs external split** — keep them distinguishable in our analytics so
339:   we never imply external traction we do not have.
340: - **Quality/latency/cost** — from the benchmark harness (P50/P90/P99 latency,
341:   cost-per-accepted-outcome, verified-rate), decision-grade only.
342: 
343: **What we may claim publicly today** (per
344: `docs/promises/2026-06-23-khala-public-copy-promise-gate-review.md` and the
345: free-tier module): Khala is a **free, live, OpenAI-compatible inference API**
346: (`openagents/khala`, base `https://openagents.com/api/v1`), with a self-serve free
347: key and a public tokens-served counter; responses carry OpenAgents receipt
348: disclosure. **What we may not claim:** that broad **paid** Khala is generally
349: launched, that any customer can fund inference end-to-end via card/Bitcoin/MPP,
350: that `khala-code` is "verified" absent an executed acceptance verdict, or that
351: Pylon contributors are paid from Khala serving without owner-armed settlement.
352: Everything in Pillars 1–3 that is "direction" (gym↔Khala, Verse↔Khala, the paid
353: three-way split, decentralized serving) stays labeled as direction until it has a
354: green promise.
355: 
356: This doc is internal strategy. The product-promise registry governs claims; the
357: companion promise review
358: (`../promises/2026-06-25-khala-inference-push-promise-review.md`) maps this push
359: onto the registry, proposes the one missing promise, and suggests what to
360: deprioritize.

(End of file - total 360 lines)

## 0. What just shipped (live, verified today)

Khala is live as an **OpenAI-compatible inference API**:

- **Endpoint:** `POST https://openagents.com/api/v1/chat/completions`, single public
  model id **`openagents/khala`** (the `khala-mini` / `khala-code` ids are not
  public products; see `../khala/README.md` and the §6228 free-tier module
  header in `apps/openagents.com/workers/api/src/inference/inference-free-tier-key.ts`).
- **Free tier, self-serve:** `POST /api/keys/free` mints a normal `oa_agent_`
  bearer key; free inference on `openagents/khala` is metered against a per-key
  per-UTC-day quota of **200 requests / 200,000 tokens** (`FREE_TIER_MAX_REQUESTS_PER_DAY`,
  `FREE_TIER_MAX_TOKENS_PER_DAY` in `inference-free-tier-key.ts`). Over-quota or
  premium lanes fall through to the normal balance + `402` gate — free within the
  quota, paid beyond it.
- **A live public "Khala Tokens Served" counter + per-day history:**
  `GET /api/public/khala-tokens-served` and
  `GET /api/public/khala-tokens-served/history` (registered in `index.ts`, backed
  by the token-usage ledger). The recorder increments on every completion;
  verified to the token under 24-wide concurrent stress. Shown on `/stats` and
  `/khala`.

This is the buy-side OpenAgents has historically never closed (the Ep 138 "lots
of sell-side, no paying demand" lesson, restated in
`2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md`). The free OpenAI-compatible API plus a
public counter is the first time we have a real, measurable demand surface that an
outsider can point an existing tool at in one config line.

## 1. The thesis

**We are now in the inference business. The one metric that matters is tokens
served per day, and we want it exponential.**

Episode 242 (`../transcripts/242.md`) named the shape: an OpenAI-compatible API
people already know how to consume, one model `openagents/khala`, free research
preview live today, an open marketplace underneath, and any paid value fanned to
contributors. The collective-intelligence essay
(`../collective-intelligence/2026-06-24-collective-intelligence-as-an-economy.md`)
named the mechanism: an **economy** selected by verifiable value, not a lab
artifact graded on its own benchmark. The inference business docs
(`2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md`,
`2026-06-19-pricing-model.md`) named the money: credits, multipliers, BTC
discount, three-way split (margin / serving node / referrer).

Tokens served per day is the metric that subsumes all of them:

- It is the **demand** proxy (the buy-side closing).
- It is the **dogfood** proxy (every internal system we route through Khala adds
  tokens).
- It is the **distribution** proxy (every ecosystem tool we land adds tokens).
- It is the **economy** proxy — once the paid loop is collectable, tokens served
  is what the three-way split is computed over.

Everything below is in service of making that one number go up, fast, **honestly**
(real served tokens, not vanity inflation), and **well** (good enough that people
keep their tools pointed at us).

We do not need to invent demand for inference — it is already paid for elsewhere
(OpenRouter, Fireworks, Together, Factory). We need to (a) be trivially adoptable,
(b) be good, and (c) generate our own demand from everything we already run.

## 2. Pillar 1 — The flywheel: plug Khala into EVERYTHING we build/run

Eat our own dog food. Each internal use is **both** dogfood (it hardens Khala and
surfaces real traffic shapes for benchmarking) **and** demand (it adds tokens to
the counter that we control and can grow on day one, before a single external
user arrives). This is the fastest, most honest lever: it is real served tokens
from real work.

Targets, in rough order of how much traffic they can move:

1. **Autonomous QA (`qa-runner` → Khala).** The autonomous QA process (the
   out-ship-Factory QA epic; the 242 draft calls QA "the first real use case")
   does real browser work and leaves green VERIFIED traces. Route its agent
   inference through `openagents/khala`. QA runs continuously, so it is a steady
   token floor and a continuous correctness signal on Khala itself. **Highest-value
   first move:** it is already running, it is internal, and it stress-tests the
   exact code/verification workload Khala is meant to be good at.
2. **OpenAgents agents (Autopilot / Raynor) use Khala.** Autopilot is the gateway's
   designated anchor buyer (`2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md` §2): every
   coding session is captive first-party demand. Raynor's forum/progress posting and
   any in-product agent reasoning should default to `openagents/khala` where the
   model fit allows. Coding is the wedge.
3. **All our products route inference through Khala.** Sites generation, forum
   agent flows, onboarding programs, the Artanis loop, Concierge — anywhere a
   product currently calls a model directly, make Khala the default lane (with the
   honest caveat that `claude`/premium lanes still route to the balance+premium
   gate, never the free lane — see the free-tier module's lane policy). One
   internal seam, many internal callers, all counted.
4. **The gym trains Khala AND uses Khala.** The training/gym loop is dual-purpose:
   it improves the model that backs `openagents/khala`, and its own agent/eval
   inference can run *through* Khala. Training that consumes the product it
   improves is the tightest possible flywheel — and it ties directly to the
   "improves, does not depreciate" claim from Ep 242. (Direction: the gym↔Khala
   wiring is not a single shipped seam today; treat as the next dogfood lane to
   build, not a live claim.)
5. **The 3D Verse visualization uses Khala.** `../khala/khala-in-the-world.md`
   renders every request to the endpoint as crackling energy fanned to assigned
   Pylons. Driving Verse NPC / scene / narration inference through Khala makes the
   visualization literally show its own traffic, and adds tokens. (Direction.)

**Why this is the right first pillar:** it is the only lever we fully control. We
can move the counter meaningfully *before* a single external developer adopts us,
and every internal token is a real test that makes the product better for the
external developers we are about to court in Pillar 2. Honesty note: internal
dogfood tokens are real served tokens and may be reported as such, but we should
be able to **distinguish** internal vs external demand in our own analytics so we
never imply external traction we do not have.

## 3. Pillar 2 — The ecosystem-tools playbook

This is exactly how every inference business grew. OpenRouter, Fireworks, and
Together did not win by being a website — they won by being a **drop-in base URL +
key + model id** in the tools developers already use, so adoption costs one config
line and zero rewrites. Our gateway is OpenAI-compatible *specifically* so this
works (`2026-06-19-inference-gateway-business.md` §4: "work by changing only the
base URL + key — zero-rewrite adoption removes friction").

The play: get `openagents/khala` listed/documented as a provider or preset in the
popular coding + agent tools, so anyone can point an existing tool at
`https://openagents.com/api/v1` and run.

### First target: OpenCode (`projects/repos/opencode`)

OpenCode is a provider-agnostic coding agent that reads a JSON config and supports
**custom OpenAI-compatible providers** via the AI SDK. Making it trivial to run
coding agents through OpenCode against our endpoint is the cleanest first landing:
it is a coding tool (our wedge), it is config-driven (no upstream PR needed to
start), and it exercises the same code/tool-calling workload Khala must be good at.

**Exact config** (verified against the OpenCode repo docs/schema —
`packages/web/src/content/docs/providers.mdx`, `config.mdx`,
`packages/core/src/config/provider.ts`). Put in `~/.config/opencode/opencode.json`
(global) or `opencode.json` (project root):

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://opencode.ai/config.json",
  "provider": {
    "openagents": {
      "npm": "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible",
      "name": "OpenAgents",
      "options": {
        "baseURL": "https://openagents.com/api/v1",
        "apiKey": "{env:OPENAGENTS_API_KEY}"
      },
      "models": {
        "openagents/khala": {
          "name": "Khala",
          "limit": { "context": 128000, "output": 65536 }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

- **Base URL:** `https://openagents.com/api/v1`. `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` POSTs
  to `<baseURL>/chat/completions`, i.e. our live `POST /api/v1/chat/completions`.
- **Key:** mint one free with `POST /api/keys/free`, export it as
  `OPENAGENTS_API_KEY`, or use OpenCode's `/connect → Other → id "openagents"` to
  store it in `~/.local/share/opencode/auth.json`. The provider id typed in
  `/connect` must equal the `provider` key in the config.
- **Model selection:** OpenCode's model reference is `providerId/modelKey`. With
  the model **key** `openagents/khala` (which is what gets sent upstream as the
  model id — correct for our API), the in-TUI selector path reads
  `openagents/openagents/khala`. **TBD to confirm in testing:** whether to keep the
  model key literally `openagents/khala` (sends the right model id; selector has a
  doubled segment) or to add server-side acceptance of a shorter key so the
  selector reads cleanly. Document whichever we choose; do not ship ambiguous
  instructions.

**What to test before we publish the OpenCode recipe:**
- The endpoint serves chat-completions at `/api/v1/chat/completions` (it does) and
  OpenCode's request reaches it with `model: "openagents/khala"`.
- **Tool/function calling** works end to end — OpenCode's edit/run loop depends on
  it. This is the single biggest risk: a model that cannot reliably tool-call is
  unusable as a coding agent regardless of token quality.
- **Streaming (SSE)** works for a good interactive experience.
- The free-tier 200 req / 200k tok daily quota is enough for a real "try it"
  session, and the `402`/quota-exceeded fall-through is a clean, legible error in
  OpenCode (not an opaque crash).
- Token accounting: an OpenCode session's tokens show up on the public
  `khala-tokens-served` counter (proving external traffic is counted).

### Next tools after OpenCode

Prioritized by how directly each is a one-config-line OpenAI-compatible drop-in
and how much coding/agent traffic it represents (reference repos under
`projects/repos/`):

1. **Aider** (`projects/repos/aider`) — CLI coding agent; OpenAI-compatible base
   URL + model env config. Big, well-known coding audience.
2. **Cline / Continue** — VS Code agent extensions with custom OpenAI-compatible
   provider config. Large IDE-native audiences.
3. **Vercel AI SDK** (`projects/repos/ai`) — `@ai-sdk/openai-compatible` provider;
   a documented snippet lets every AI-SDK app point at us. This is the substrate
   under OpenCode and many others, so a clean AI-SDK recipe is high-leverage.
4. **LiteLLM / LangChain provider lists** — getting `openagents/khala` into the
   community provider/config lists (LiteLLM `model_list`, LangChain
   `ChatOpenAI(base_url=...)`) reaches the long tail of agent frameworks.
5. **Codex / Claude Code-compatible clients** — any client that already speaks the
   OpenAI Chat Completions shape and accepts a base URL override (per the gateway
   business doc's named clients).
6. **OpenRouter-style aggregators** — listing Khala as an upstream model in
   aggregators that fan out to many providers (later-stage; needs reliability +
   the paid loop).

For each tool the deliverable is the same: a short, exact, copy-pasteable recipe
(base URL + free key + model id) plus a "what to test" checklist, published where
the tool's users look. Keep the recipes in this repo's docs and on `/khala` once
the copy gate clears.

## 4. Pillar 3 — Benchmarking ("make it good," not just "get there")

Adoption dies if the model is not good. We need a repeatable benchmarking process
that measures Khala's **quality + latency + cost** against other tools/models, so
we can improve it deliberately and so our public comparisons are receipts, not
vibes.

We already have the foundation. The typed, fixture-driven, no-spend benchmark
harness shipped under
`apps/openagents.com/workers/api/src/inference/benchmark/`
(`../khala/2026-06-23-khala-benchmark-harness-book-p1-5.md`): a declarative
matrix (lane × engine × workload × sequence-shape × transport × verification
outcome), a runner with a fixture lane (deterministic, spend-free) and an
**owner-gated** real lane (`makeRealLaneSeam`, default OFF, refuses to spend
unarmed), and a public-safe dereferenceable report (latency percentiles,
**cost-per-accepted-outcome**, verification rate, cache hit rate). The book's
lesson is baked in: "faster" is meaningless until you say faster at *what*, on
*which lane*, under *which traffic*, judged on *which outcome*; read latency in
P50/P90/P99, not the mean; and a benchmark is only decision-grade when an
owner-armed real seam runs over **realistic** traffic.

What the GTM push needs on top of that:

- **An owner-armed real sweep.** Replace synthetic shapes with shapes sourced from
  observed Khala traffic (the dogfood from Pillar 1 *is* that traffic), arm
  `makeRealLaneSeam` with a live executor under a budget cap, and produce the first
  `decisionGrade: true` report comparing Khala vs Fireworks/Vertex on chat /
  khala-code / verifier / long-context. This is the existing Open Question #5
  minimum decision suite, run for real. (Owner-gated; not done in this doc.)
- **External head-to-head comparisons.** Extend beyond our own lanes to compare
  `openagents/khala` against the tools/models developers would otherwise use, on
  the same prompts, reporting tokens, $, wall-clock, **and** our unique axes
  (cost-per-accepted-outcome, verified-rate). This is the buildout roadmap's
  north-star "Fugu-vs-frontier"-style head-to-head
  (`../khala/khala-buildout-roadmap.md`), but generalized into a recurring quality
  bar we can publish.

### Run it through the gym — the benchmark ladder

The **gym** is where this runs: the same gym that trains and uses Khala (Pillar 1)
also **benchmarks** it on a recurring basis, so every training or serving change is
automatically re-scored. The gym drives the harness above over a *ladder of
opponents*, all on identical prompts and our axes (tokens, $, wall-clock,
cost-per-accepted-outcome, verified-rate):

1. **BigPickle vs Khala** — the first named baseline target. *(Confirm exactly
   which model/benchmark "BigPickle" refers to before wiring this rung; the rest
   of the ladder doesn't depend on it.)*
2. **Other (open / free) models vs Khala** — the open models a developer would
   otherwise reach for (the catalog/ecosystem set), so we know where Khala stands
   among the free options people compare us to.
3. **Paid (frontier) models vs Khala** — the paid Claude/GPT/Gemini-class models as
   the upper bar, so we can see the gap to frontier and track it closing over time.

The progression is deliberate: prove Khala against the baseline and the **free**
field first (where we must win on cost-per-accepted-outcome and verified-rate),
then measure the climb toward **paid** frontier. The gym publishes the ladder as a
recurring, dereferenceable leaderboard — and per the honesty bar below, numbers are
only published from the owner-armed real seam over realistic traffic
(`decisionGrade: true`).

Honesty bar for any published benchmark number: it must come from the owner-armed
real seam over realistic traffic (`decisionGrade: true`); fixture/synthetic runs
are explicitly labeled illustrative and never published as measurements.

## 5. Sequence

1. **Internal dogfood demand (Pillar 1), starting now.** Route qa-runner →
   Khala, then Autopilot/Raynor, then the rest of our products. This moves the
   counter immediately and generates the realistic traffic the benchmark needs.
   It is the only step that depends on nobody but us.
2. **OpenCode integration (Pillar 2, first target).** Verify and publish the exact
   one-config recipe + test checklist. First external "point your tool at us" win.
3. **Benchmark harness / gem (Pillar 3).** Confirm the owner's benchmark+competitor
   names; arm a real sweep over the now-real dogfood traffic; produce the first
   decision-grade report and a publishable head-to-head.
4. **Broaden to more tools (Pillar 2 continued).** Aider → Cline/Continue → Vercel
   AI SDK → LiteLLM/LangChain lists → aggregators. One recipe at a time, each with
   a test checklist.
5. **Keep driving internal dogfood demand throughout.** Pillar 1 never stops; new
   internal systems default to Khala as they ship. Constant motion.

These overlap (1 and 2 can run in parallel; 1 feeds 3), but the *gating* order is:
internal demand and OpenCode do not block on the paid loop or the registry, so
they ship first; the paid three-way-split economics are owner-gated and sequence
behind proof.

## 6. Metrics + honesty

**The north-star metric is tokens served per day** — read off the live
`/api/public/khala-tokens-served` counter and its `/history`, the same numbers
shown on `/stats` and `/khala`. We want the per-day history curve to bend upward
and stay up. Supporting metrics:

- **Per-tool adoption** — tokens attributable to OpenCode, Aider, etc. (so we know
  which ecosystem landings actually move traffic).
- **Internal vs external split** — keep them distinguishable in our analytics so
  we never imply external traction we do not have.
- **Quality/latency/cost** — from the benchmark harness (P50/P90/P99 latency,
  cost-per-accepted-outcome, verified-rate), decision-grade only.

**What we may claim publicly today** (per
`docs/promises/2026-06-23-khala-public-copy-promise-gate-review.md` and the
free-tier module): Khala is a **free, live, OpenAI-compatible inference API**
(`openagents/khala`, base `https://openagents.com/api/v1`), with a self-serve free
key and a public tokens-served counter; responses carry OpenAgents receipt
disclosure. **What we may not claim:** that broad **paid** Khala is generally
launched, that any customer can fund inference end-to-end via card/Bitcoin/MPP,
that `khala-code` is "verified" absent an executed acceptance verdict, or that
Pylon contributors are paid from Khala serving without owner-armed settlement.
Everything in Pillars 1–3 that is "direction" (gym↔Khala, Verse↔Khala, the paid
three-way split, decentralized serving) stays labeled as direction until it has a
green promise.

This doc is internal strategy. The product-promise registry governs claims; the
companion promise review
(`../promises/2026-06-25-khala-inference-push-promise-review.md`) maps this push
onto the registry, proposes the one missing promise, and suggests what to

236: // COMPONENT-CHANNEL CONFIG (issue #6127). `enabled` is the gateway-level flag
237: // (default off). When on, the channel is STILL only activated per-request via an
238: // explicit opt-in for a Khala model. `repairReask` (optional) wires the ONE
239: // bounded repair turn over a single non-streaming Khala call; absent => an
240: // invalid card is dropped without a repair attempt (still never shipped).
241: export type ComponentChannelConfig = Readonly<{
242:   enabled: boolean
243:   repairReask?: ComponentRepairReask | undefined
244: }>

246: // Resolve whether the typed component channel is active FOR THIS REQUEST. Three
247: // AND-gated conditions, all required, so the default `/v1` shape never changes:
248: //   1. the gateway-level flag is on (`config.enabled`)
249: //   2. the request explicitly opts in (header `x-oa-component-channel: on`, or a
250: //      truthy `oa_component_channel` body field) — a deterministic parse of an
251: //      explicit caller-supplied switch, never an intent inference
252: //   3. the model is a Khala model (the channel is a Khala capability)
253: export const resolveComponentChannelActive = (
254:   input: Readonly<{
255:     config: ComponentChannelConfig | undefined
256:     request: Request
257:     rawBody: Record<string, unknown>
258:     requestedModel: string
259:     autopilotConcierge?: boolean | undefined
260:   }>,
261: ): boolean => {
262:   if (input.config?.enabled !== true) {
263:     return false
264:   }
265:   if (!isKhalaModel(input.requestedModel)) {
266:     return false
267:   }
268:   if (input.autopilotConcierge === true) {
269:     return true
270:   }
271:   const header = input.request.headers
272:     .get('x-oa-component-channel')
273:     ?.trim()
274:     .toLowerCase()
275:   const headerOptIn =
276:     header !== undefined && ['1', 'true', 'yes', 'on'].includes(header)
277:   const bodyField = input.rawBody['oa_component_channel']
278:   const bodyOptIn =
279:     bodyField === true ||
280:     (typeof bodyField === 'string' &&
281:       ['1', 'true', 'yes', 'on'].includes(bodyField.trim().toLowerCase()))
282:   return headerOptIn || bodyOptIn
283: }

291:   registry: InferenceProviderRegistry
292:   // Single-id router seam. Defaults to the stub router (always selects the
293:   // stub/echo adapter). Used to gate `model_unavailable` and to dispatch when
294:   // no multi-lane `lanePlan` is supplied (#5476 path).
295:   router?: ModelRouter
296:   // Ordered multi-lane plan for cheapest-viable selection + overflow (#5482).
297:   // When present, the route dispatches across the plan with bounded-backoff
298:   // overflow on retryable failures. The Worker wires this to `selectAdapterPlan`.
299:   lanePlan?: ModelLanePlanner
300:   // PROVIDER SERVING POLICY (blocker.product_promises.public_paid_model_gateway_missing
301:   // on api.hosted_gemini.v1). The SAME presence-derived lane arming the public
302:   // catalog (`/v1/models`) and the pre-purchase quote (`/v1/quote`) are gated on.
303:   // When supplied, a request for a KNOWN model whose supply lane is NOT armed is
304:   // rejected with a clean `model_unavailable` (400) BEFORE any account-state gate
305:   // or provider dispatch — so the gateway serves exactly what it advertises and
306:   // quotes, instead of accepting the request and failing deep at dispatch with a
307:   // generic `provider_error` (502). An UNKNOWN model id is not gated (the
308:   // estimator prices it at the conservative fallback rate, consistent with
309:   // `/v1/quote`). Omitting it preserves the prior serve-everything behaviour.
310:   // Presence-only; no secret value is read here.
311:   laneArming?: SupplyLaneArming
312:   // Routing overflow knobs (backoff + injected sleep) forwarded to
313:   // `dispatchWithOverflow`. Tests inject `sleep: () => Effect.void` so overflow
314:   // never waits. Ignored unless `lanePlan` is supplied.
315:   dispatch?: Omit<DispatchDeps, 'registry' | 'plan'>
316:   // Defaults to the no-op/log metering stub. The Worker supplies the live
317:   // ledger hook (`makeLedgerMeteringHook`, #5477) when the gateway is enabled.
318:   meteringHook?: MeteringHook
319:   // SERVED-TOKENS RECORDER (issue #6227). Records one canonical
320:   // `token_usage_events` row per SERVED completion so the public "Khala Tokens
321:   // Served" counter (GET /api/public/khala-tokens-served) reflects ALL Khala
322:   // traffic. Invoked AFTER the metering hook on every completion path
323:   // (non-streaming, buffered stream, true pass-through stream) and only when the
324:   // request produced real provider usage — so a failed / 4xx / refused call is
325:   // never recorded. It runs INDEPENDENTLY of which metering wrapper handled the
326:   // request, so a FREE-TIER completion (whose credit-ledger hook is
327:   // short-circuited in `withFreeTierKhala`) STILL records its served tokens (the
328:   // tokens were served and must count, even though no credit was debited). The
329:   // row is idempotent per request id (one served completion = one row); a
330:   // retry/replay is a no-op insert. Default undefined => no-op (the inert/test
331:   // path is byte-for-byte unchanged). The recorder never throws and never fails
332:   // the customer's already-delivered completion.
333:   recordTokensServed?: ServedTokensRecorder | undefined
334:   // Resolves the account's funding kind (card | bitcoin) for the metering hook.
335:   // Defaults to card (no Bitcoin discount).
336:   resolveFundingKind?: InferenceFundingResolver
337:   // Minimum available balance (msat) required to accept a request. Until #5477
338:   // prices per-model, any positive balance clears the gate; an account with
339:   // zero/negative available balance is rejected.
340:   minimumAvailableMsat?: number
341:   // FREE-ALLOWANCE PRE-FLIGHT (EPIC #5474 §1). Read-only mirror of the gate
342:   // inside `withFreeAllowance`. The balance gate calls this BEFORE rejecting a
343:   // zero/insufficient-balance account: if the (account, model) is free-eligible
344:   // and the resolving owner still has remaining free allowance, the request is
345:   // allowed through (the metering hook then eats the cost and accrues it), so a
346:   // genuinely-free request is never falsely 402'd. Default undefined => the gate
347:   // is unchanged (a zero-balance account is rejected). Resolution errors return
348:   // not-eligible, so the balance gate stands. Wired by the Worker to
349:   // `checkFreeAllowancePreflight` against the SAME owner-identity resolver the
350:   // metering hook uses, keeping the bypass and the accrual consistent.
351:   checkFreeAllowance?: (
352:     accountRef: string,
353:     model: string,
354:   ) => Promise<{ readonly eligible: boolean }>
355:   // OWNER BALANCE-GATE EXEMPTION (issue #6180). Read-only seam the balance gate
356:   // calls BEFORE the 402: if the (account, model) resolves to an EXEMPT verified
357:   // owner AND the model is non-premium / own-infra, the zero-balance request is
358:   // allowed through (the `withOperatorCredit` metering wrapper then records it as
359:   // `operator_credit` — zero credit debit + receipt, no referral). Lets
360:   // approved/internal keys test our OWN lanes (e.g. `openagents/khala` on the
361:   // hourly Hydralisk box) WITHOUT funding while Khala stays paid for the public.
362:   // HARD GUARDRAIL: a PREMIUM model (`claude`/`unknown`) is NEVER exempt — it
363:   // still hits the normal balance + premium gates. Default undefined => the gate
364:   // is unchanged (a zero-balance account is rejected). Resolution errors return
365:   // not-exempt, so the balance gate stands. Wired by the Worker to
366:   // `makeOperatorExemptionGate` ONLY when INFERENCE_OPERATOR_EXEMPTION_ENABLED is
367:   // on, against the SAME owner-identity resolver the metering wrapper uses so the
368:   // bypass and the zero-debit record agree.
369:   checkOperatorExemption?: (
370:     accountRef: string,
371:     model: string,
372:   ) => Promise<{ readonly exempt: boolean }>
373:   // KHALA FREE TIER bypass (issue #6228). Read-only seam the balance gate calls
374:   // BEFORE the 402: if the (account, model) is a FREE-TIER key on the free Khala
375:   // lane (own-infra / non-premium) AND still within its per-key daily quota, the
376:   // zero-balance request is allowed through (the `withFreeTierKhala` metering
377:   // wrapper then records it as a zero-debit free receipt + accrues the quota).
378:   // This is the THIRD balance-gate bypass alongside `checkFreeAllowance` (owner
379:   // Sybil pool on Gemini) and `checkOperatorExemption` (owner operator credit);
380:   // it is the self-serve one — anyone who mints a free key gets free Khala within
381:   // quota. A PREMIUM model is NEVER free; over-quota / non-free-tier / non-Khala
382:   // requests fall through to the normal 402. Default undefined => the gate is
383:   // unchanged. Wired by the Worker to `makeFreeTierGate` ONLY when
384:   // INFERENCE_FREE_TIER_ENABLED is on, against the SAME store + quota the metering
385:   // wrapper uses so the bypass and the zero-debit accrual agree.
386:   checkFreeTier?: (
387:     accountRef: string,
388:     model: string,
389:   ) => Promise<{ readonly free: boolean }>
390:   // ABUSE-CONTROL SEAMS (#5486). Both default to undefined => the gate is OPEN
391:   // (no-op), so the inert/flag-off path and the unconfigured-account path are
392:   // byte-for-byte unchanged. The decisions are computed by the pure deciders in
393:   // inference-abuse-controls.ts (`decideFairShare` / `decideSpendCap`); the
394:   // Worker wires these to per-account window counters (D1/KV) so one customer
395:   // cannot starve the shared Vertex quota (fair-share) or drain its whole balance
396:   // via a compromised key (spend cap), both DISTINCT from the raw balance gate.
397:   //
398:   // PER-CUSTOMER RATE / FAIR-SHARE GATE. Returns the fair-share decision for the
399:   // account in the current window. When `allowed` is false the route rejects with
400:   // the decision's statusCode (429) and sets RateLimit-* headers from the
401:   // bounded counters. Checked AFTER auth (so it is keyed to the account) and
402:   // BEFORE provider dispatch (so a starve-attempt never reaches a provider).
403:   checkFairShare?: (accountRef: string) => Promise<FairShareDecision>
404:   // PER-ACCOUNT SPEND-CAP GATE. Returns the spend-cap decision for the account in
405:   // the current window. When `allowed` is false the route rejects with the
406:   // decision's statusCode (402, distinct from the balance gate's
407:   // insufficient_credits). Checked after the balance gate; pre-flight it bounds
408:   // the already-spent window total (no per-request estimate exists yet).
409:   checkSpendCap?: (accountRef: string) => Promise<SpendCapDecision>
410:   // PREMIUM-MODEL OWNER-GRANT GATE (free-tier enablement §2). Returns the
411:   // premium-access decision for the (account, model) pair. Premium models
412:   // (Claude / GPT / partner-passthrough) require the account's resolved OWNER
413:   // identity to be on the owner-controlled allowlist; non-allowlisted premium
414:   // requests are DENIED (403) with a clear, actionable message. Non-premium
415:   // models (Gemini free default, Fireworks open) always pass. Default undefined
416:   // => the gate is OPEN (no-op), so the inert/flag-off path is unchanged.
417:   // Checked AFTER auth (so it is keyed to the resolved owner) and BEFORE
418:   // provider dispatch (so a denied premium request never reaches a provider).
419:   checkPremiumAccess?: (
420:     accountRef: string,
421:     model: string,
422:   ) => Promise<PremiumAccessDecision>
423:   // Deterministic id/timestamp injection for tests. `nowEpochSeconds` defaults
424:   // to the runtime-primitives clock; `newId` to a runtime-primitives random id.
425:   nowEpochSeconds?: () => number
426:   newId?: () => string
427:   // DURABLE-STREAM RANK-1 SEAM (#6058, EPIC #6056). When `durableStreamEnabled`
428:   // is true AND `durableStream` resolves a per-request `StreamStore`, a streaming
429:   // completion is teed into a durable offset log keyed by the response id, so a
430:   // client disconnect mid-generation can be resumed by offset (the durable read
431:   // route). DEFAULT OFF + UNWIRED: with the flag off or no store factory, the
432:   // streaming path is byte-for-byte today's pass-through (no persistence/resume).
433:   // Metering still settles EXACTLY ONCE on the real upstream EOF and NEVER on a
434:   // resume/replay read (the resume route has no metering hook).
435:   durableStreamEnabled?: boolean | undefined
436:   durableStream?: DurableInferenceStreamStore | undefined
437:   // PRODUCTION DURABLE SUBSTRATE (#6058). When present AND `durableStreamEnabled`
438:   // is true, the streaming completion is teed into the per-request Durable Object
439:   // (`DurableInferenceStreamObject`, keyed `getByName(responseId)`) over the
440:   // `/v1/stream/{id}` HTTP contract — the DO is the single authoritative durable
441:   // log a LATER GET resume reads. This is the live wiring; `durableStream` (the
442:   // synchronous `StreamStore` factory) is the in-memory test/contract substrate.
443:   // When both are present the DO namespace wins. Absent (e.g. the binding is
444:   // unbound on an env) => fail-safe non-durable pass-through.
445:   durableStreamNamespace?: DurableStreamNamespace | undefined
446:   // TYPED COMPONENT CHANNEL SEAM (EPIC #6123, issue #6127). The ADDITIVE,
447:   // OPT-IN `oa.component` SSE channel: a Khala turn may stream prose PLUS one or
448:   // more validated, versioned `oa.component` cards from the CLOSED v1 catalog
449:   // (khala-component-channel.ts). DEFAULT OFF: with `componentChannel` absent or
450:   // `enabled: false`, `/v1/chat/completions` is byte-for-byte today's text-only
451:   // stream — standard OpenAI clients are unaffected. When `enabled` is true the
452:   // channel is STILL only activated for a request that explicitly opts in (the
453:   // `x-oa-component-channel: on` header or an `oa_component_channel: true` body
454:   // field) AND targets a Khala model, so the default response shape never
455:   // changes. On activation, the gateway:
456:   //   - injects the closed-catalog system prompt (stable prefix),
457:   //   - assembles the completion, splits prose vs fenced `oa-component` blocks,
458:   //   - validates each card's props against the closed catalog with Effect
459:   //     Schema (ONE bounded repair turn, then drop — never ship malformed),
460:   //   - honors the SAME provider-identity redaction backstop as the prose path,
461:   //   - re-emits prose as `{content}` deltas and each card as one atomic
462:   //     `event: oa.component` frame.
463:   componentChannel?: ComponentChannelConfig | undefined
464:   // CACHE-AWARE ROUTING SEAM (book P0-2 deliverable 6 / #6084). When wired, a
465:   // same-session/codebase/account follow-up is routed to the cache-WARM lane
466:   // first (the lane that previously served this affinity hash), subject to the
467:   // lane still being viable + healthy + privacy/region-allowed. All three are
468:   // INJECTED capabilities so routing stays pure + typed (no ad-hoc string
469:   // matching). DEFAULT UNWIRED: with `cacheWarmthOracle` absent the lane plan is
470:   // unchanged (cheapest-viable order), so existing behavior is unaffected.
471:   //   - `cacheWarmthOracle` : affinity HASH → the lane that last served it.
472:   //   - `laneHealthOracle`  : lane → health posture (warm hint ignored if sick).
473:   //   - `cachePinPolicy`    : lane → may this account pin here (privacy/region).
474:   cacheWarmthOracle?: CacheWarmthOracle | undefined
475:   laneHealthOracle?: LaneHealthOracle | undefined
476:   cachePinPolicy?: CachePinPolicy | undefined
477:   // Deterministic clock (epoch ms) for the durable log's TTL/offset bookkeeping.
478:   // Defaults to a fixed value in tests; the Worker threads `currentEpochMillis`.
479:   nowEpochMillis?: (() => number) | undefined
480:   // ACCEPTANCE-DISPATCH SEAM (EPIC #6017). When a khala-code completion produces an
481:   // EXECUTABLE artifact (it passes the cheap pre-screen), the gateway enqueues an
482:   // out-of-Worker verification job: a node-side runner (Pylon / sandbox / Cloud Run)
483:   // runs the real headless acceptance suite and posts the verdict back to the
484:   // authenticated callback, which backfills the receipt (`unverified` ->
485:   // `test_passed`/`failed`). DEFAULT OFF + UNWIRED: with `acceptanceDispatch.enabled`
486:   // false or no `queue`/`artifactStore` wired, NOTHING is enqueued and the receipt
487:   // stays the honest `unverified`. The Worker wires this once a runner host is
488:   // deployed and the flag (KHALA_ACCEPTANCE_DISPATCH_ENABLED) is on. Chromium NEVER
489:   // runs in the Worker; this only enqueues a job (pure producer).
490:   acceptanceDispatch?: Readonly<{
491:     enabled: boolean
492:     queue: AcceptanceJobQueue | undefined
493:     // Persist the artifact bytes and return a dereferenceable ref the runner
494:     // resolves (an R2 key). Absent => no job is enqueued (the runner would have no
495:     // artifact to fetch). Receives the request id + the runnable HTML.
496:     storeArtifact?: (
497:       input: Readonly<{ requestId: string; html: string }>,
498:     ) => Promise<string>
499:   }>
500:   // CROSS-APP TRACE EMISSION (openagents #6214, epic #6206). When wired AND
501:   // enabled AND the request opts in, a COMPLETED non-streaming Khala chat session
502:   // is mapped to a public-safe ATIF trajectory and persisted via the shared trace
503:   // store, so the session becomes a shareable `/trace/{uuid}`. DEFAULT OFF +
504:   // UNWIRED: with `traceEmit` absent or `enabled: false`, NOTHING is emitted and
505:   // `/v1/chat/completions` is byte-for-byte today's behaviour. The emit is
506:   // fire-and-forget over the already-delivered completion (it never blocks, fails,
507:   // or alters the response). The emitted model id is the gateway projection
508:   // (`openagents/khala`), never a raw backend; the emitter reuses the SAME ingest
509:   // validation + public-safety tripwire (never bypassed). Autopilot/Pylon
510:   // emission are explicit follow-ups (#6214), not built on this path.
511:   traceEmit?: Readonly<{
512:     enabled: boolean
513:     // Persist a completed Khala chat session as an ATIF trace. The Worker wires
514:     // this to `emitKhalaChatTrace` against the shared trace store + the resolved
515:     // owner. Absent => no-op. Returns void; the call site logs nothing sensitive.
516:     emit: (
517:       input: Readonly<{
518:         accountRef: string
519:         requestedModel: string
520:         requestMessages: ReadonlyArray<InferenceMessage>
521:         result: InferenceResult
522:         responseId: string
523:         optedIn: boolean
524:       }>,
525:     ) => Promise<void>
526:   }>
527: }>
528: 
529: // REQUEST SCHEMA ----------------------------------------------------------
530: type ChatCompletionsRequestBody = Readonly<{
531:   // `model` is OPTIONAL: an unspecified model defaults to DEFAULT_CHAT_MODEL
532:   // (`openagents/khala`) in `resolveRequestedModel` below.
533:   model?: string | undefined
534:   messages: ReadonlyArray<InferenceMessage>
535:   stream?: boolean | undefined
536: }>
537: 
538: // Resolve the effective requested model: a present, non-blank `model` normalized
539: // through the single Khala alias rule, otherwise the Khala default. Centralized
540: // so the default applies uniformly to routing, premium gating, response echo,
541: // and metering.
542: export const resolveRequestedModel = (model: string | undefined): string => {
543:   const trimmed = model?.trim()
544:   return trimmed === undefined || trimmed === ''
545:     ? DEFAULT_CHAT_MODEL
546:     : normalizeKhalaModelId(trimmed)
547: }
548: 
549: const decodeOpenAiContent = (value: unknown): string | undefined => {
550:   if (typeof value === 'string') {
551:     return value
552:   }
553:   if (value === null) {
554:     return ''
555:   }
556:   if (!Array.isArray(value)) {
557:     return undefined
558:   }
559:   const parts: Array<string> = []
560:   for (const item of value) {
561:     const record = recordFromUnknown(item)
562:     if (record?.['type'] !== 'text' || typeof record['text'] !== 'string') {
563:       return undefined
564:     }
565:     parts.push(record['text'])
566:   }
567:   return parts.join('\n\n')
568: }
569: 
570: const nonEmptyString = (value: unknown): string | undefined =>
571:   typeof value === 'string' && value !== '' ? value : undefined
572: 
573: const decodeMessage = (value: unknown): InferenceMessage | undefined => {
574:   const record = recordFromUnknown(value)
575:   const role = nonEmptyString(record?.['role'])
576:   if (record === undefined || role === undefined) {
577:     return undefined
578:   }
579:   const rawToolCalls = record['tool_calls']
580:   const toolCalls =
581:     rawToolCalls === undefined
582:       ? undefined
583:       : inferenceToolCallsFromUnknown(rawToolCalls)
584:   if (rawToolCalls !== undefined && toolCalls === undefined) {
585:     return undefined
586:   }
587:   const content =
588:     record['content'] === undefined && toolCalls !== undefined
589:       ? ''
590:       : decodeOpenAiContent(record['content'])
591:   if (content === undefined) {
592:     return undefined
593:   }
594:   const toolCallId = nonEmptyString(record['tool_call_id'])
595:   const name = nonEmptyString(record['name'])
596:   return {
597:     content,
598:     ...(name === undefined ? {} : { name }),
599:     role,
600:     ...(toolCallId === undefined ? {} : { toolCallId }),
601:     ...(toolCalls === undefined || toolCalls.length === 0
602:       ? {}
603:       : { toolCalls }),
604:   }
605: }
606: 
607: const decodeBody = (
608:   value: unknown,
609: ): ChatCompletionsRequestBody | undefined => {
610:   const record = recordFromUnknown(value)
611:   if (record === undefined || !Array.isArray(record['messages'])) {
612:     return undefined
613:   }
614:   const model = record['model']
615:   if (model !== undefined && typeof model !== 'string') {
616:     return undefined
617:   }
618:   const stream = record['stream']
619:   if (stream !== undefined && typeof stream !== 'boolean') {
620:     return undefined
621:   }
622:   const messages = record['messages'].map(decodeMessage)
623:   if (messages.some(message => message === undefined)) {
624:     return undefined
625:   }
626:   return {
627:     messages: messages as ReadonlyArray<InferenceMessage>,
628:     ...(model === undefined ? {} : { model }),
629:     ...(stream === undefined ? {} : { stream }),
630:   }
631: }
632: 
633: // GATEWAY-SIDE STABLE PROMPT LAYOUT (book P0-2 / #6084 deliverable 1+2). Build the
634: // outgoing messages as TAGGED blocks so the prefix-cache assembler can order them
635: // STABLE content first (acceptance contract → identity → tool schemas → stable
636: // policy) and NOVEL/volatile content last — the book's rule "novel tokens as late
637: // as possible" so the long shared prefix stays cacheable. Each gateway-injected
638: // block is tagged with its `StableBlockKind`, so ordering is deterministic and
639: // structural (classification of OUR OWN injected blocks, not a keyword match on
640: // user intent — honors the workspace semantic-routing rule).
641: //
642: // Identity injection (STEP 1, the PRIMARY identity mechanism): for every
643: // `openagents/khala-*` request the strong Khala identity system message is part of
644: // the STABLE prefix so the identity rule binds to every Khala consumer and the
645: // post-completion signature guard remains the backstop on top.
646: //
647: // Acceptance-contract injection (EPIC #6017): for a khala-code coding request whose
648: // intent maps to an executable acceptance lane, the contract guidance (the runner's
649: // `window` hooks) is a STABLE prefix block too. Both are additive; the assembler
650: // orders them canonically (acceptance contract leads, then identity) regardless of
651: // append order.
652: const buildTaggedKhalaMessages = (
653:   clientMessages: ReadonlyArray<InferenceMessage>,
654:   requestedModel: string,
655:   autopilotConcierge: AutopilotConciergeRequestConfig | undefined,
656:   // COMPONENT-CHANNEL (issue #6127): inject the closed-catalog system prompt as a
657:   // STABLE prefix block ONLY when the channel is active for this request. Absent /
658:   // false => no injection, prompt byte-identical to before (additive + opt-in).
659:   componentChannelActive?: boolean,
660: ): ReadonlyArray<TaggedPromptMessage> => {
661:   const tagged: Array<TaggedPromptMessage> = []
662: 
663:   // Acceptance-contract stable block (khala-code coding lane only).
664:   if (requestedModel === KHALA_CODE_MODEL_ID) {
665:     const guidance = acceptanceContractGuidanceForRequest({
666:       messages: clientMessages.map(message => ({
667:         content: message.content,
668:         role: message.role,
669:       })),
670:       model: requestedModel,
671:     })
672:     if (guidance !== undefined) {
673:       tagged.push({
674:         message: { content: guidance, role: 'system' },
675:         stableKind: 'acceptanceContract' satisfies StableBlockKind,
676:       })
677:     }
678:   }
679: 
680:   // Identity stable block (every khala-* model).
681:   if (isKhalaModel(requestedModel)) {
682:     tagged.push({
683:       message: { content: KHALA_IDENTITY_SYSTEM_PROMPT, role: 'system' },
684:       stableKind: 'identity' satisfies StableBlockKind,
685:     })
686:   }
687: 
688:   // Autopilot Concierge product prompt (#6148): server-owned model config,
689:   // vertical enum, component catalog metadata, and Output Spec instructions. It
690:   // is injected by the gateway for the virtual model only; callers cannot supply
691:   // arbitrary vertical/system-prompt text.
692:   if (autopilotConcierge !== undefined) {
693:     tagged.push({
694:       message: {
695:         content: buildAutopilotConciergeSystemPrompt(autopilotConcierge),
696:         role: 'system',
697:       },
698:       stableKind: 'otherSystem' satisfies StableBlockKind,
699:     })
700:   }
701: 
702:   // Component-catalog stable block (issue #6127): only when the typed component
703:   // channel is active for this Khala request. The catalog prompt lists the closed
704:   // v1 component set + prop shapes so the model surfaces a card via the fenced
705:   // `oa-component` mechanism that works across all Khala backends.
706:   if (componentChannelActive === true && isKhalaModel(requestedModel)) {
707:     tagged.push({
708:       message: { content: KHALA_COMPONENT_CATALOG_PROMPT, role: 'system' },
709:       stableKind: 'identity' satisfies StableBlockKind,
710:     })
711:   }
712: 
713:   // Client messages: their own `system` messages are stable policy/steer (the
714:   // assembler classifies role==='system' as `otherSystem`, ordered after the
715:   // known stable blocks); everything else is volatile/novel (ordered last).
716:   for (const message of clientMessages) {
717:     tagged.push({ message })
718:   }
719:   return tagged
720: }
721: 
722: const toInferenceRequest = (
723:   body: ChatCompletionsRequestBody,
724:   raw: Record<string, unknown>,
725:   requestedModel: string,
726:   // The session-affinity passthrough params derived from the request's
727:   // cache-affinity key (book P0-2 deliverable 4). MERGED into passthroughParams
728:   // (overriding any stray client copy) so the adapters pin the session to one
729:   // cache-warm replica. Empty for non-Khala / no-affinity requests.
730:   affinityParams: Readonly<Record<string, string>>,
731:   // COMPONENT-CHANNEL (issue #6127): when active, the closed-catalog system prompt
732:   // is injected as a stable prefix block. Default false => no injection.
733:   componentChannelActive?: boolean,
734:   autopilotConcierge?: AutopilotConciergeRequestConfig | undefined,
735: ): InferenceRequest => {
736:   const clientMessages: ReadonlyArray<InferenceMessage> = body.messages
737:   // For a non-Khala model, leave the messages exactly as the client sent them
738:   // (no gateway injection, no reordering) — byte-identical to prior behavior.
739:   // For a Khala model, assemble the cache-optimal stable layout.
740:   const messages = isKhalaModel(requestedModel)
741:     ? assembleStablePromptLayout(
742:         buildTaggedKhalaMessages(
743:           clientMessages,
744:           requestedModel,
745:           autopilotConcierge,
746:           componentChannelActive,
747:         ),
748:       ).messages
749:     : clientMessages
750:   const { messages: _messages, model: _model, stream: _stream, ...rest } = raw
751:   return {
752:     messages,
753:     model: requestedModel,
754:     // Gateway-derived session affinity wins over any stray client copy.
755:     passthroughParams: { ...rest, ...affinityParams },
756:     stream: body.stream === true,
757:   }
758: }
759: 
760: // Resolve the cache-affinity dimensions for a request from the authenticated
761: // account plus client-supplied session / codebase hints (book P0-2 deliverable
762: // 3). The session/codebase hints are read from the OpenAI-style `user` field and
763: // a non-standard `codebase` hint in the raw body, plus the `x-session-affinity` /
764: // `x-codebase` request headers — all OPTIONAL, all bounded fields (deterministic
765: // parse of explicit caller-supplied identifiers, never an intent parse). Only
766: // `account` is required; the others sharpen affinity when present.
767: const resolveCacheAffinity = (
768:   accountRef: string,
769:   raw: Record<string, unknown>,
770:   request: Request,
771: ): Readonly<{
772:   rawKey: string
773:   hash: string
774:   params: Readonly<Record<string, string>>
775: }> => {
776:   const stringField = (value: unknown): string | undefined =>
777:     typeof value === 'string' && value.trim() !== '' ? value.trim() : undefined
778:   const header = (name: string): string | undefined => {

## Project Conventions

- Read `DESIGN.md` before changing UI. OpenAgents Autopilot uses the Vortex dark,
  mono, operational design direction; do not introduce light mode, decorative
  gradients, card-heavy marketing shells, or generic chat-first framing.
- When writing GitHub issue comments with `gh`, never pass Markdown containing
  escaped `\n` sequences in a quoted `--body` string. Use real newlines via
  `--body-file -`, a heredoc/stdin body, or a body variable populated from a
  heredoc. For important issue comments, verify the rendered body with
  `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/issues/comments/<comment-id> --jq .body` before
  moving on.
- Before editing a UI surface for a named URL, trace the actual rendered
  entrypoint from routing/startup through the top-level view and verify which
  component produces the live DOM. Do not assume that a same-named file is the
  route owner. For example, live `/login` is rendered by `apps/web/src/view.ts`
  via the GitHub OAuth login button, while
  `apps/web/src/page/loggedOut/page/login.ts` is a legacy/local auth view and
  is not the production `/login` surface. When changing deployed UI, verify
  against the served bundle or rendered DOM before calling the work complete.
- When the user asks to deploy this repo, always build the latest web assets
  before deploying the Worker. For JS/CSS/public-doc/web-asset-only changes,
  prefer the no-container path in
  `docs/2026-06-15-openagents-web-deploy-runbook.md`: run
  `bun run check:deploy`, run `bun run build:web`, then deploy from
  `apps/openagents.com/workers/api` with
  `npx wrangler deploy --containers-rollout=none --assets ../../apps/web/dist`
  so Wrangler does not build or update Containers. Use
  `bun run --cwd workers/api deploy` for full production deploys that need its

## Pylon Presence Auth Contract (token-only)

Pylon presence and lifecycle writes (`POST /api/pylons/:ref/heartbeat`,
`/wallet-readiness`, `/payout-target-admission`, `/assignments/*`, and
`/register`) are authenticated with an OpenAgents agent **bearer token**
(`Authorization: Bearer <agent token>`) in `workers/api/src/pylon-api-routes.ts`
(`requireAgent`). This is intentional and is the documented contract.

A node's self-held Nostr key (NIP-98) is **not** accepted as presence
authority. Registrations are bound to `ownerAgentUserId` derived from the
bearer-token session; the registry does not bind a verified Nostr pubkey to
that owner (`providerNostrPubkey` is optional discovery metadata, only set for
NIP-90 provider Pylons, and is never verified server-side). Accepting a
self-signed heartbeat would require server-side NIP-98 schnorr verification
plus a mandatory pubkey→owner binding — a broader auth change that must not be
made implicitly.

When a presence request arrives with a `Nostr`/NIP-98 `Authorization` scheme
and no usable bearer token, the route returns a typed, explanatory `401`
(`error: pylon_api_presence_requires_agent_token`, `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer`)
naming the token-only contract and pointing the node at the bearer path,
rather than a bare `unauthorized` (#5058). Do not silently fall back to a bare
401 for the Nostr-signed presence path. If self-signed presence is ever to be
supported, design the NIP-98 verification + pubkey binding deliberately with a
new dated review and tests; do not broaden any other authority (spend,
settlement, moderation) in the process. Regression coverage lives in
`workers/api/src/pylon-api-routes.test.ts`.

## Foldkit Patterns

### Update

`init` and `update` both return `[Model, ReadonlyArray<Command<Message>>]`:

```ts
type UpdateReturn = readonly [Model, ReadonlyArray<Command<Message>>]
const withUpdateReturn = M.withReturnType<UpdateReturn>()

const update = (model: Model, message: Message): UpdateReturn =>
  M.value(message).pipe(
    withUpdateReturn,
    M.tagsExhaustive({
      ClickedIncrement: () => [evo(model, { count: count => count + 1 }), []],
    }),
  )
```

Use `evo()` from `foldkit/struct` for immutable model updates. Never spread or `Object.assign`.

### View

Bind the `html` factory inside each view function with `const h = html<Message>()` (never at module level), then reach for `h.div`, `h.OnClick`, etc. off the returned record. Use `empty` (not `null`) for conditional rendering, `M.value().pipe(M.tagsExhaustive({...}))` for discriminated unions, and `Array.match` for lists that may be empty.

Use `keyed` wrappers whenever the view branches into structurally different layouts based on route or model state. Without keying, the virtual DOM tries to diff one layout into another, causing stale DOM and event handler mismatches.

### Commands

Define a Command with `Command.define`, which is curried: the first call binds the name (and optionally args + result Message schemas), and the second call binds the Effect. Assign definitions to PascalCase constants. Never inline in pipe chains. Commands catch all errors via `Effect.catch(() => Effect.succeed(FailedX(...)))` so side effects never crash the app. Definitions live colocated with the update function that returns them.

For the with-args shape, see `../projects/repos/foldkit/examples/weather/src/main.ts` or `../projects/repos/foldkit/examples/kanban/src/command.ts`. For an argless DOM-side-effect Command, the argless form in `kanban/src/command.ts` (`FocusAddCardInput`) is the canonical reference.

For DOM operations (focus, scroll, modals, scroll lock), Foldkit ships a `Dom` module. For time, randomness, UUIDs, and delays, use Effect's built-ins directly (`Clock`, `Random`, `Effect.uuid`, `Effect.sleep`). Don't reach for raw `document.querySelector`, `setTimeout`, `Date.now()`, or `Math.random()`.

### File Organization

This repo is a Bun workspace:

- `apps/web/` is the Foldkit/Vite browser app.
- `workers/api/` is the Cloudflare Worker API and Durable Object surface.
- `packages/sync-schema/` holds Effect Schema protocol models shared by browser and Worker code.
- `packages/sync-client/` holds browser-side sync helpers.
- `packages/sync-worker/` holds Worker-side sync helpers.

Keep Cloudflare runtime code out of `apps/web/`. Keep browser/Foldkit code out
of `workers/api/`. Shared code belongs in `packages/*` and must stay Effect
Schema-first at every external boundary.

A Foldkit app lives in two files. `src/main.ts` holds the pure definitions (Model, Messages, init, update, view). `src/entry.ts` imports them and boots the runtime with `Runtime.makeProgram` and `Runtime.run`. `index.html` references `entry.ts`. The split keeps `main.ts` importable from tests without booting a runtime as a side effect. Never call `Runtime.run` from `main.ts`.

Use uppercase section headers (`// MODEL`, `// MESSAGE`, `// INIT`, `// UPDATE`, `// COMMAND`, `// VIEW`) for wayfinding.

### Testing

Test update functions with `foldkit/test`. Since update is pure, tests run without a runtime, DOM, or side effects. Use `Story.story` for update-level tests (send Messages, assert on Model and Commands) and `Scene.scene` for feature-level testing through the view with accessible locators. Study the `.story.test.ts` and `.scene.test.ts` files in `../projects/repos/foldkit/examples/` when the external Foldkit reference exists.

## Code Style

- Encode state in discriminated unions, not booleans or nullable fields. `Idle | Loading | Error | Ok`, not `isLoading: boolean`. Make impossible states unrepresentable.
- Use `Option` instead of `null` or `undefined`. Prefix Option-typed values with `maybe*`. Match with `Option.match`; don't unwrap with `Option.map(...)` + `Option.getOrElse(...)` when you can just match.
- Use Effect modules over native methods in `pipe` chains (`Array.map`, `String.startsWith`, `Array.findFirst`). Native methods are fine when calling directly on a named variable.
- Never cast Schema values with `as Type`. Use the callable constructor: `SucceededLogin({ sessionId })`, not `{ _tag: 'SucceededLogin', sessionId } as Message`.
- Always `Array.isEmptyArray` / `Array.isNonEmptyArray` (not `.length === 0`). Use `Array.match` when handling both empty and non-empty cases.
- Never use `for` loops or `let` for iteration. Reach for `Array.map`, `Array.filterMap`, `Array.makeBy`, `Array.reduce`.
- Never use `T[]`. Always `Array<T>` or `ReadonlyArray<T>`.
- Always use `Effect.Match`, never `switch`.
- Always use braces for control flow: `if (foo) { return true }`.
- Don't add inline comments to explain code. Use better names instead. Reserve `// NOTE:` for behavior that would mislead a careful reader.

## Message Layout

Group all `m()` declarations together with no blank lines between them, then put `S.Union([...])` and `type Message = typeof Message.Type` on adjacent lines:

```ts
const ClickedSubmit = m('ClickedSubmit')
const UpdatedEmail = m('UpdatedEmail', { value: S.String })

const Message = S.Union([ClickedSubmit, UpdatedEmail])
type Message = typeof Message.Type
```

Messages are verb-first past-tense. Common prefixes: `Clicked*`, `Updated*` (input changes and external state updates), `Submitted*`, `Pressed*`, `Selected*`, `Succeeded*` / `Failed*` (paired async results), `Completed*` (fire-and-forget), `Got*` (child OutMessage in the Submodel pattern).

## Debugging

This project ships with `@foldkit/devtools-mcp` pre-wired. When the dev server is running and the app is open in a browser, `foldkit_*` MCP tools let you inspect Model, Message history, and time-travel. Reach for them before adding `console.log` whenever the question is about state or Message flow.

## Going Deeper

For Submodels and OutMessage, Subscriptions, Mount / ManagedResource / CustomElement, field validation, routing, accessibility, and the full convention set, read the live Foldkit code in `../projects/repos/foldkit/`. The `examples/` directory and the production apps (`packages/typing-game/`, `packages/website/`) are the highest-fidelity references for any specific pattern. The `foldkit-skills` plugin's `generate-program` and `audit-program` skills carry written snapshot guides if you want a structured walkthrough.
